examples.explanation: Start at index 0 (value 2). Try jump 1: to index 1 (value 3). From index 1: Try jump 1: to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: Try jump 1: to index 3 (value 1). From index 3: Try jump 1: to index 4 (last index). Reached last index → return true. Backtrack shows other paths also possible but first found path suffices.

Start at index 0 (value 3). Try jump 1: to index 1 (value 2). From index 1: Try jump 1: to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: Try jump 1: to index 3 (value 0). From index 3: Cannot jump (value 0) → dead end. Backtrack to index 2: Try jump 2: exceeds array bounds. Backtrack to index 1: Try jump 2: to index 3 (value 0) → dead end. Backtrack to index 0: Try jump 2: to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: Try jump 1: to index 3 (value 0) → dead end. Try jump 2: exceeds bounds. Backtrack to index 0: Try jump 3: to index 3 (value 0) → dead end. All paths exhausted → return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible jump sequences from each index, checking if any path reaches the last index. This is a naive approach that exhaustively tries every combination of jumps.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Backtracking for Jump Game
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all possible jump sequences to determine if last index is reachable
ladders.explanation: The brute force backtracking approach solves the Jump Game problem by exhaustively exploring every possible sequence of jumps from the starting position. The intuition is that if we can reach the last index through any path, the answer is true; otherwise false. We treat each position as a decision point where we can jump 1 to nums[i] steps forward. The algorithm starts at index 0 and recursively tries all possible jumps from each position. At each recursive call, we check if we've reached or passed the last index (base case success). If we're at a position with jump distance 0, we've hit a dead end (base case failure). Otherwise, we try every possible jump length from 1 to nums[current] and recursively explore each path. The time complexity is O(2^n) because at each position with value k, we branch into k recursive calls, leading to exponential growth. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This approach connects to L1 by using the recursive exploration as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling the case where current position equals last index (should return true), forgetting to check bounds when making jumps, and not optimizing with memoization (though that would change the approach). The brute force nature makes it impractical for large inputs but valuable for understanding the problem structure.

examples.trace: Start at index 0 (value 2). Try jump 1: to index 1 (value 3). From index 1: Try jump 1: to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: Try jump 1: to index 3 (value 1). From index 3: Try jump 1: to index 4 (last index). Reached last index → return true. Backtrack shows other paths also possible but first found path suffices.

Start at index 0 (value 3). Try jump 1: to index 1 (value 2). From index 1: Try jump 1: to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: Try jump 1: to index 3 (value 0). From index 3: Cannot jump (value 0) → dead end. Backtrack to index 2: Try jump 2: exceeds array bounds. Backtrack to index 1: Try jump 2: to index 3 (value 0) → dead end. Backtrack to index 0: Try jump 2: to index 2 (value 1). From index 2: Try jump 1: to index 3 (value 0) → dead end. Try jump 2: exceeds bounds. Backtrack to index 0: Try jump 3: to index 3 (value 0) → dead end. All paths exhausted → return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Jump Exploration from Current Position
ladders.desc: Function that recursively tries all possible jumps from a given index to reach the last index
ladders.explanation: This subroutine forms the core of the brute force approach. The intuition is that from any position i, we need to determine if we can reach the last index by making jumps of length 1 to nums[i]. The function takes the current index and the nums array as parameters. Base cases: if current index >= last index, return true (we've reached or passed the target). If nums[current] == 0, return false (dead end). Recursive case: for jump length from 1 to nums[current], make a recursive call to canJumpFrom(current + jump). If any recursive call returns true, propagate true upward. If all jumps lead to false, return false. Time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(branching_factor^depth) where branching factor varies by nums[i] values. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L0 as the main recursive engine, and to L2 which focuses on the branching logic. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in jump ranges (should be 1 to nums[current] inclusive), not checking if current >= last index before checking nums[current] == 0, and not using OR logic to combine results. The subroutine demonstrates how backtracking explores all possibilities.

examples.trace: Current index 0, last index 2. nums[0] = 2. Try jump 1: recursive call from index 1. From index 1: nums[1] = 0, current index 1 < last index 2 → return false. Back to index 0: Try jump 2: recursive call from index 2. From index 2: current index 2 >= last index 2 → return true. Propagate true upward.
Current index 1, last index 3. nums[1] = 0, current index 1 < last index 3 → return false (dead end). No jumps possible.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Branching on All Possible Jump Lengths
ladders.desc: The decision process of trying every jump distance from 1 to current position's value
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the branching behavior essential to brute force exploration. The intuition is that at each position i with jump capacity nums[i], we must consider all possible forward movements. The algorithm: given current index i and value k = nums[i], we iterate jump from 1 to k. For each jump length, we move to position i + jump and continue exploration. This creates a decision tree where each node branches into up to k children. The key insight is that we must try ALL possibilities because we don't know which jump length (if any) will lead to success. Time complexity for this branching step at a single position is O(k) where k = nums[i]. In the full recursion, these branches multiply. Space complexity for this step is O(1) aside from recursion. This connects to L1 as the recursive expansion mechanism, and to L3 which handles array bounds. Common mistakes include starting jump from 0 (jump of 0 doesn't progress), using jump <= nums[i] instead of jump <= nums[i] (off-by-one), and not handling when i + jump exceeds array bounds (should be caught in base case). This logic demonstrates exhaustive search principles.

examples.input: At index 0 with nums[0] = 3, array length 5
examples.trace: Current position: index 0, value 3. Possible jumps: 1 (to index 1), 2 (to index 2), 3 (to index 3). Each becomes a new recursive starting point. No jump of 0 or 4+ considered.

examples.input: At index 2 with nums[2] = 0, array length 4
examples.trace: Current position: index 2, value 0. Jump range: 1 to 0 is empty. No branches created → dead end.

testCases.expected: Branches to indices 1, 2 (3+ exceed bounds but handled later)

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Array Bounds and Termination Conditions
ladders.desc: Determining when we've reached the end or gone out of bounds
ladders.explanation: This building block handles the conditions that stop recursion. The intuition is that we need clear rules for when to stop exploring: either we've succeeded (reached/passed last index), failed (stuck at position with 0 jump capacity before reaching end), or gone out of bounds. The algorithm checks: if current index >= last index (where last index = array length - 1), return true (success). This covers both exact landing and overshooting. If current index < last index and nums[current] == 0, return false (dead end). If we attempt a jump that would go beyond array bounds, that recursive path will immediately hit the success condition if it reaches or passes last index, or will be invalid if it goes too far (though in this problem, jumps beyond array are treated as reaching end). Time complexity O(1), space O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the stopping criteria for branches, and to L4 which accesses array elements. Common mistakes include using current index > last index instead of >= (overshooting should succeed), checking nums[current] == 0 before checking if current >= last index (if at last index with value 0, should return true), and not defining last index clearly. These conditions ensure the recursion terminates.

examples.trace: Check: current index 3 >= last index 3 → true, return success immediately without checking nums[3].
Check: current index 2 < last index 4, and nums[2] == 0 → dead end, return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Element and Jump Value
ladders.desc: Reading the maximum jump distance from current position in array
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves retrieving the jump capacity from the nums array. The intuition is simple: at position i, the value nums[i] tells us how far we can jump from that position. The algorithm: given index i and array nums, access nums[i] to get an integer k. This k represents the maximum jump length; we can jump 1 to k steps forward. Time complexity O(1), space O(1). This connects to L3 by providing the value needed for termination checks, and to L5 which covers array basics. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices (i must be 0 <= i < len(nums)), misunderstanding that nums[i] is the maximum jump (not fixed jump), and not storing the value before recursion (though can be accessed in loop). In Python-style pseudocode: jump_capacity = nums[current_index]. This operation is repeated at every recursive call, forming the data flow of the algorithm.

examples.trace: Access nums[0] → value 2. This means from index 0, we can jump up to 2 steps forward.
Access nums[2] → value 5. From index 2, maximum jump is 5 steps.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Zero-based Counting
ladders.desc: Understanding how to access elements in an array using indices starting from 0
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the basics of array indexing, which is prerequisite for the Jump Game problem. The intuition: an array is a collection of elements stored at consecutive positions. Each position has an index, starting from 0 for the first element. To access an element, we use the syntax array[index]. For an array of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. The last index is n-1, not n. This zero-based counting is crucial because jump distances are calculated relative to current index. For example, if at index i and we jump j steps, we land at index i + j. If the array has length 5, indices are 0,1,2,3,4. Last index is 4. Time complexity O(1) for access, space O(1). This connects to L4 as the mechanism for accessing nums[i]. Common mistakes include thinking index starts at 1, using array length as last index (off-by-one), and not understanding that i + j can equal n (which means reached/passed last index). Practice: given array [a,b,c,d], index 0 = a, index 1 = b, index 2 = c, index 3 = d, length = 4, last index = 3.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30. Index 1 accesses the second element, value 20.
Array length = 1, indices: 0→5. Last index = length - 1 = 0.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store whether each index can reach the last index, avoiding redundant calculations. This improves over brute force by caching results for subproblems.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to determine if you can reach the last index from the first index.
ladders.explanation: The Jump Game problem asks whether you can reach the last index of an array starting from the first index, where each element represents the maximum jump length from that position. The DP memoization approach solves this by breaking it into overlapping subproblems: for each index i, we ask 'can I reach the last index from here?' We use recursion to explore all possible jumps from i (up to nums[i] steps), and memoize results to avoid redundant calculations. The intuition is that if we can reach the last index from any position reachable from i, then we can reach it from i. This is a top-down DP approach that improves brute force O(2^n) to O(n^2) by caching. We define a recursive function canReach(i) that returns True if index i can reach the last index, False otherwise. We memoize results in an array memo where memo[i] stores the result for index i (initialized to -1 for unknown). The base case is when i == n-1 (last index), return True. For each i, we iterate j from 1 to nums[i] (jump lengths), and if i+j is within bounds and canReach(i+j) returns True, we set memo[i] = True and return. If no jump leads to success, memo[i] = False. The main function calls canReach(0). Time is O(n^2) worst-case as each index may check up to n jumps, and space is O(n) for recursion stack and memo array. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine canReach() recursively. Common mistakes include not handling base cases correctly, off-by-one errors in jump ranges, or not memoizing properly leading to exponential time. Real code pattern: def canJump(nums): n=len(nums); memo=[-1]*n; def canReach(i): if i==n-1: return True; if memo[i]!=-1: return memo[i]; for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): if i+j<n and canReach(i+j): memo[i]=True; return True; memo[i]=False; return False; return canReach(0).

examples.trace: Start at index 0 with nums[0]=2. Call canReach(0): memo[0] is -1, not last index. Check jumps: j=1 to 2. For j=1: i+j=1, call canReach(1). canReach(1): memo[1] is -1, not last. nums[1]=3, check jumps j=1 to 3. j=1: i+j=2, call canReach(2). canReach(2): memo[2] is -1, not last. nums[2]=1, check j=1: i+j=3, call canReach(3). canReach(3): memo[3] is -1, not last. nums[3]=1, check j=1: i+j=4, call canReach(4). canReach(4): i=4 is last index, return True. Back to canReach(3): set memo[3]=True, return True. Back to canReach(2): set memo[2]=True, return True. Back to canReach(1): set memo[1]=True, return True. Back to canReach(0): set memo[0]=True, return True. Final output true.

Start at index 0 with nums[0]=3. Call canReach(0): memo[0] is -1. Check jumps j=1 to 3. j=1: i+j=1, call canReach(1). canReach(1): memo[1] is -1, nums[1]=2, check j=1 to 2. j=1: i+j=2, call canReach(2). canReach(2): memo[2] is -1, nums[2]=1, check j=1: i+j=3, call canReach(3). canReach(3): memo[3] is -1, nums[3]=0, check jumps j=1 to 0 (none), so set memo[3]=False, return False. Back to canReach(2): no other jumps, set memo[2]=False, return False. Back to canReach(1): try j=2: i+j=3, call canReach(3) which returns False (memoized). No jumps lead to True, set memo[1]=False, return False. Back to canReach(0): try j=2: i+j=2, call canReach(2) returns False. j=3: i+j=3, call canReach(3) returns False. All jumps fail, set memo[0]=False, return False. Final output false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that determines if a given index can reach the last index, with memoization to cache results.
ladders.explanation: The key subroutine is the recursive function canReach(i) that returns a boolean indicating whether index i can reach the last index of the array. This function embodies the DP principle: solve subproblems (can I reach from i?) and reuse results. The intuition is that we can answer the question for i by checking all indices reachable from i in one jump; if any of those can reach the last index, then i can too. We use memoization to store results in an array memo, where memo[i] is -1 (unknown), True, or False. This avoids recalculating the same subproblem multiple times, turning exponential time into polynomial. Step-by-step: Base case: if i is the last index, return True. Check memo: if memo[i] is not -1, return its value. Otherwise, for each jump length j from 1 to nums[i] (inclusive), compute next index = i + j. If next index is within array bounds and canReach(next) returns True, set memo[i] = True and return True. If no jump leads to success, set memo[i] = False and return False. Time analysis: In worst case, each call to canReach(i) may make up to nums[i] recursive calls, but with memoization, each index is computed once, leading to O(n^2) total due to nested loops in recursion. Space is O(n) for memo and recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called recursively in the full solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check bounds for i+j, incorrect base case handling (e.g., returning False for last index), or not initializing memo properly. Real code pattern: def canReach(i): if i == n-1: return True; if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): if i+j < n and canReach(i+j): memo[i] = True; return True; memo[i] = False; return False.

examples.trace: Call canReach(1): i=1 is not last index (4). memo[1] is -1. nums[1]=3, so check jumps j=1 to 3. j=1: i+j=2, call canReach(2). canReach(2): i=2 not last, memo[2] is -1, nums[2]=1, check j=1: i+j=3, call canReach(3). canReach(3): i=3 not last, memo[3] is -1, nums[3]=1, check j=1: i+j=4, call canReach(4). canReach(4): i=4 is last, return True. Back to canReach(3): set memo[3]=True, return True. Back to canReach(2): set memo[2]=True, return True. Back to canReach(1): set memo[1]=True, return True.

Call canReach(2): i=2 not last (4). memo[2] is -1. nums[2]=1, check j=1: i+j=3, call canReach(3). canReach(3): i=3 not last, memo[3] is -1, nums[3]=0, so no jumps (range 1 to 0 empty). Set memo[3]=False, return False. Back to canReach(2): no other jumps, set memo[2]=False, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Memoization in Recursion
ladders.desc: Understand how to cache results of subproblems in a recursive function to avoid redundant calculations.
ladders.explanation: Memoization is a technique where we store the results of expensive function calls and return the cached result when the same inputs occur again. In the context of the Jump Game, the core logic is to apply memoization to the recursive exploration of paths. The intuition is that many indices will be visited multiple times in a naive recursion (e.g., from different starting points), so by caching whether an index can reach the end, we compute each subproblem only once. This transforms the time complexity from exponential to polynomial. We use an array memo of size n, initialized to a sentinel value (e.g., -1) meaning 'not computed yet'. When the recursive function is called with index i, we first check memo[i]; if it's not -1, we return the cached value immediately. Otherwise, we compute the result by exploring jumps, and before returning, store the result in memo[i]. This ensures that subsequent calls with the same i use the cached value. Step-by-step: Initialize memo array. In recursive function: if memo[i] is set, return it; else compute, set memo[i], and return. Time analysis: Without memoization, recursion could be O(2^n) as each index branches. With memoization, each index is computed once, and each computation involves iterating up to nums[i] jumps, so O(n^2) worst-case. Space is O(n) for memo. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the canReach function. Common mistakes: Using a sentinel that conflicts with possible return values (e.g., using 0 for False), not updating memo before returning, or using a global variable incorrectly. Real code pattern: memo = [-1]*n; def canReach(i): if memo[i] != -1: return memo[i]; # compute result; memo[i] = result; return result.

examples.input: nums=[2,3,1,1,4], compute canReach(2) after canReach(3) is memoized
examples.trace: Suppose memo array initially all -1. First, canReach(3) is called from somewhere: it computes that from index 3 with nums[3]=1, jumping to index 4 (last) gives True, so sets memo[3]=True and returns True. Later, when canReach(2) is called: check memo[2] is -1, so compute. nums[2]=1, jump to index 3. Instead of recursing, it checks memo[3] which is True, so canReach(2) sets memo[2]=True and returns True without further recursion.

examples.input: nums=[3,2,1,0,4], compute canReach(1) after canReach(2) is memoized
examples.trace: Memo initially all -1. Suppose canReach(2) was computed earlier: from index 2 with nums[2]=1, jump to index 3, which fails (as nums[3]=0), so memo[2]=False. Now canReach(1) is called: memo[1] is -1, compute. nums[1]=2, check jumps: j=1 to 2. j=1: jump to index 2, check memo[2] is False, so skip. j=2: jump to index 3, need to compute canReach(3) which will set memo[3]=False. Since no jump succeeds, set memo[1]=False and return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion with Array Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function that explores possible jumps from an index, ensuring not to go out of array bounds.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves writing a recursive function that, given an index i and an array nums, explores all indices reachable from i by jumping up to nums[i] steps. The key is to handle array bounds correctly to avoid index errors. The intuition is to simulate the jumps: from position i, you can move to i+1, i+2, ..., i+nums[i], but only if these indices are within the array length n. This recursion forms the basis of the DP approach, as it defines the subproblem structure. Step-by-step: Define a function explore(i) that returns a list of reachable indices or a boolean if we reach the end. Base case: if i is the last index, return success. Otherwise, for j from 1 to nums[i], compute next = i + j. If next is less than n (i.e., within bounds), recursively call explore(next). We don't use memoization here yet. Time analysis: In worst case, this can lead to exponential calls if we explore all paths without caching, but for this sub-problem, we focus on the recursion structure. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L2: This recursion is enhanced with memoization in the core logic. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check i+j < n, using zero-based indexing incorrectly, or infinite recursion if nums[i] is large. Real code pattern: def explore(i): if i == n-1: return True; for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): next_idx = i + j; if next_idx < n: if explore(next_idx): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Call explore(0): i=0 not last. nums[0]=2, so j=1 to 2. j=1: next_idx=1, call explore(1). explore(1): i=1 not last, nums[1]=3, j=1 to 3. j=1: next_idx=2, call explore(2). explore(2): i=2 not last, nums[2]=1, j=1: next_idx=3, call explore(3). explore(3): i=3 not last, nums[3]=1, j=1: next_idx=4, call explore(4). explore(4): i=4 is last, return True. Back to explore(3): returns True. Back to explore(2): returns True. Back to explore(1): returns True. Back to explore(0): returns True.

Call explore(0): i=0 not last. nums[0]=1, j=1: next_idx=1, call explore(1). explore(1): i=1 not last, nums[1]=0, so no jumps (range 1 to 0 empty), return False. Back to explore(0): no other jumps, return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Possible Jumps from an Index
ladders.desc: Given an index and a maximum jump length, generate all valid next indices within array bounds.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves computing the set of indices that can be reached from a current index i in one jump, based on the value nums[i]. The intuition is simple: from position i, you can jump to i+1, i+2, ..., i+nums[i], but you must ensure these indices are within the array length n to avoid errors. This operation is fundamental because it defines the transitions in the Jump Game. Step-by-step: Input: index i, array nums, length n. Output: list of indices reachable from i. Initialize an empty list reachable. For jump length j from 1 to nums[i] (inclusive), compute next_idx = i + j. If next_idx < n, add next_idx to reachable. If nums[i] is 0, the loop runs from 1 to 0, so no indices are added. Time analysis: O(nums[i]) time per index, which in worst case is O(n) if nums[i] is large. Space is O(nums[i]) for the list. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the recursive exploration. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., using j from 0 to nums[i]-1, which would include i itself), not checking bounds, or misunderstanding that nums[i] is the maximum jump, not exact jump. Real code pattern: def get_reachable(i, nums, n): reachable = []; for j in range(1, nums[i]+1): next_idx = i + j; if next_idx < n: reachable.append(next_idx); return reachable.

examples.trace: i=0, nums[0]=2. Loop j from 1 to 2: j=1: next_idx=0+1=1, check 1<5 true, add 1. j=2: next_idx=0+2=2, check 2<5 true, add 2. End loop, reachable list is [1,2].
i=3, nums[3]=0. Loop j from 1 to 0: since start 1 > end 0, loop does not execute. Reachable list is empty [].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Loops
ladders.desc: Understand how to access array elements by index and iterate over a range of numbers.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers basic array operations: accessing an element at a given index and looping through a sequence of numbers. In programming, an array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index (usually starting from 0). For the Jump Game, we need to read nums[i] to know the maximum jump length from index i. Looping is used to iterate over possible jump lengths. The intuition is that arrays allow random access, and loops enable repetitive actions. Step-by-step: To access an element: given array nums and index i, use nums[i]. Ensure i is within bounds: 0 <= i < length of nums. To loop over a range: for j in range(start, end): executes for j from start to end-1. For example, range(1, nums[i]+1) gives j = 1, 2, ..., nums[i]. Time analysis: Access is O(1), looping over k items is O(k). Space is O(1) for variables. Connection to L4: This is used to compute reachable indices. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing when language uses 0-based, off-by-one in range (e.g., range(0, nums[i]) would give 0 to nums[i]-1), or not checking bounds leading to index out of range errors. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); for i in range(n): value = nums[i]; # access element. For looping: for j in range(1, 5): # j from 1 to 4.

examples.input: nums=[2,3,1,1,4], access index 2, loop from 1 to nums[2]
examples.trace: Array nums has indices 0 to 4. Access nums[2]: at index 2, value is 1. Now, loop from 1 to nums[2]+1 = 1 to 2: range(1,2) gives j=1 only (since end is exclusive). So loop runs once with j=1.

examples.input: nums=[3,2,1,0,4], access index 0, loop from 1 to nums[0]
examples.trace: Access nums[0]: value is 3. Loop from 1 to nums[0]+1 = 1 to 4: range(1,4) gives j=1,2,3. So loop runs three times.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Tabulation O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Iteratively build a DP table from the end to the start, marking indices as reachable if they can jump to a reachable index. This bottom-up approach eliminates recursion overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with DP Tabulation O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using bottom-up DP to determine if you can reach the last index from the first.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Jump Game problem solved using dynamic programming with tabulation. The intuition is to work backwards from the end of the array to determine which indices can reach the last index. We create a DP array where dp[i] indicates whether index i can reach the last index (n-1). We initialize dp[n-1] = True since the last index can reach itself. Then, for each index i from n-2 down to 0, we check if i can jump to any index j where i < j ≤ i+nums[i] and dp[j] is True. If such a j exists, then dp[i] = True, meaning we can reach the end from i. The final answer is dp[0]. This bottom-up approach avoids recursion overhead and builds the solution systematically. The time complexity is O(n²) because for each i, we may check up to n indices in the worst case. Space complexity is O(n) for the DP array. Connection to L1: This level implements the complete algorithm using the reachability check subroutine from L1. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the jump range or incorrectly initializing the DP array. Real code pattern: def canJump(nums): n = len(nums); dp = [False]*n; dp[n-1] = True; for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): max_jump = min(i+nums[i], n-1); for j in range(i+1, max_jump+1): if dp[j]: dp[i] = True; break; return dp[0]

examples.trace: nums = [2,3,1,1,4], n=5. Initialize dp = [F,F,F,F,F], set dp[4]=True. i=3: nums[3]=1, max_jump=min(3+1,4)=4. Check j=4: dp[4]=True → dp[3]=True. i=2: nums[2]=1, max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. Check j=3: dp[3]=True → dp[2]=True. i=1: nums[1]=3, max_jump=min(1+3,4)=4. Check j=2: dp[2]=True → dp[1]=True. i=0: nums[0]=2, max_jump=min(0+2,4)=2. Check j=1: dp[1]=True → dp[0]=True. Return dp[0]=True.

nums = [3,2,1,0,4], n=5. Initialize dp = [F,F,F,F,F], set dp[4]=True. i=3: nums[3]=0, max_jump=min(3+0,4)=3. Check j=4: j=4 > max_jump=3, so no check → dp[3]=False. i=2: nums[2]=1, max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. Check j=3: dp[3]=False; j=4: j=4 > max_jump=3, so no check → dp[2]=False. i=1: nums[1]=2, max_jump=min(1+2,4)=3. Check j=2: dp[2]=False; j=3: dp[3]=False → dp[1]=False. i=0: nums[0]=3, max_jump=min(0+3,4)=3. Check j=1: dp[1]=False; j=2: dp[2]=False; j=3: dp[3]=False → dp[0]=False. Return dp[0]=False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reachability Check from Current Index
ladders.desc: Check if a given index can reach any reachable index within its jump range.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the DP solution: for a given index i, determine if it can reach the last index by checking all indices within its jump range. The intuition is that if index i can jump to any index j that is already known to reach the last index, then i can also reach the last index. The algorithm takes the current index i, the nums array, and the dp array (which stores reachability from each index to the end). It computes max_jump = min(i+nums[i], n-1) to handle jumps beyond the array bounds. Then, it iterates j from i+1 to max_jump (inclusive) and checks if dp[j] is True. If found, it returns True; otherwise, False. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(k) where k is the jump length at index i, up to O(n) in worst case. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each i in the main loop to fill the DP table. Common mistakes include not handling the upper bound correctly or starting j from i instead of i+1. Real code pattern: def can_reach_from_i(i, nums, dp): n = len(nums); max_jump = min(i+nums[i], n-1); for j in range(i+1, max_jump+1): if dp[j]: return True; return False

examples.trace: n=5, i=1, nums[1]=3, max_jump=min(1+3,4)=4. Check j=2: dp[2]=False; j=3: dp[3]=False; j=4: dp[4]=True → return True.
n=5, i=3, nums[3]=0, max_jump=min(3+0,4)=3. Check j=4: j=4 > max_jump=3, so no iteration → return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bottom-up DP Table Filling
ladders.desc: Fill a DP table from the end to start to determine reachability.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core DP technique: building a table from the base case backwards. The intuition is that by starting from the last index (which is trivially reachable) and moving leftwards, we can use previously computed results to determine reachability for earlier indices. The algorithm initializes a DP array dp of size n with all False, and sets dp[n-1] = True. Then, it iterates i from n-2 down to 0. For each i, it checks if i can jump to any index j where dp[j] is True (using the logic from L1). If yes, set dp[i] = True. After the loop, dp[0] gives the answer. This is bottom-up because we solve smaller subproblems (indices closer to the end) first. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops. Space complexity is O(n). Connection to L1: This level uses the reachability check as a black box to fill the DP table. Common mistakes include incorrect loop direction or not initializing the base case. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); dp = [False]*n; dp[n-1] = True; for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): if can_reach_from_i(i, nums, dp): dp[i] = True; return dp[0]

examples.trace: n=5, dp init=[F,F,F,F,F], set dp[4]=T. i=3: check reachable → T, dp[3]=T. i=2: check → T, dp[2]=T. i=1: check → T, dp[1]=T. i=0: check → T, dp[0]=T. Final dp=[T,T,T,T,T].
n=5, dp init=[F,F,F,F,F], set dp[4]=T. i=3: check → F, dp[3]=F. i=2: check → F, dp[2]=F. i=1: check → F, dp[1]=F. i=0: check → F, dp[0]=F. Final dp=[F,F,F,F,T].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Backwards with Jump Range Calculation
ladders.desc: Loop from the end of an array and compute valid jump ranges for each index.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two key operations: iterating backwards through an array and calculating the maximum reachable index for each position. The intuition is that in DP problems, we often process data in reverse to build upon solved subproblems. The algorithm takes an array nums and for each index i from n-1 down to 0, computes max_jump = min(i+nums[i], n-1). This ensures we don't attempt to jump beyond the array bounds. Then, we can use this max_jump in further checks. This is a building block because it sets up the structure for the DP solution without implementing the full logic. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop. Space complexity is O(1) if just computing values. Connection to L2: This level provides the loop and range calculation needed to fill the DP table. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop range or not handling the min() correctly. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); for i in range(n-1, -1, -1): max_jump = min(i+nums[i], n-1); # Use max_jump for further operations

examples.output: i=4: max_jump=4; i=3: max_jump=4; i=2: max_jump=3; i=1: max_jump=4; i=0: max_jump=2
examples.trace: n=5. i=4: nums[4]=4, max_jump=min(4+4,4)=4. i=3: nums[3]=1, max_jump=min(3+1,4)=4. i=2: nums[2]=1, max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. i=1: nums[1]=3, max_jump=min(1+3,4)=4. i=0: nums[0]=2, max_jump=min(0+2,4)=2.

examples.output: i=4: max_jump=4; i=3: max_jump=3; i=2: max_jump=3; i=1: max_jump=3; i=0: max_jump=3
examples.trace: n=5. i=4: nums[4]=4, max_jump=min(4+4,4)=4. i=3: nums[3]=0, max_jump=min(3+0,4)=3. i=2: nums[2]=1, max_jump=min(2+1,4)=3. i=1: nums[1]=2, max_jump=min(1+2,4)=3. i=0: nums[0]=3, max_jump=min(0+3,4)=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Access array elements and ensure indices are within valid range.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves reading values from an array using indices and preventing index out-of-bounds errors. The intuition is that in algorithms, we frequently need to access elements at specific positions, and we must ensure those positions exist within the array's length to avoid runtime errors. The algorithm takes an array arr and an index i. First, check if 0 ≤ i < len(arr). If valid, access arr[i]; otherwise, handle the error (e.g., return a default value or adjust i). In the Jump Game context, we use this to get nums[i] and to ensure jumps don't exceed array bounds by computing min(i+nums[i], n-1). This is a basic operation because it's a prerequisite for any array manipulation. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used when calculating max_jump and accessing dp values. Common mistakes include forgetting to check bounds or using incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: def safe_access(arr, i): n = len(arr); if 0 <= i < n: return arr[i]; else: return None # or handle error

examples.trace: n=5, i=2 is between 0 and 4 inclusive → valid. Return arr[2]=1.
n=5, i=5 is not < 5 → invalid. Return None or raise IndexError.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand what an array is and how to access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: an array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient random access to elements based on their position. In programming, arrays are zero-indexed in languages like Python, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To access an element, use the syntax arr[i] where i is the index. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. The length of an array is the number of elements, accessible as len(arr). Valid indices range from 0 to len(arr)-1. This foundation is crucial because the Jump Game problem uses arrays to store jump distances. Time and space analysis are not applicable here as it's a basic concept. Connection to L4: This level provides the fundamental knowledge needed to perform array operations safely. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: arr = [2, 3, 1]; first_element = arr[0]; # 2 length = len(arr); # 3

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0=5, index 1=8, index 2=2. Accessing arr[1] returns 8.
Array has 2 elements: 100 at index 0 and 200 at index 1. len(arr) returns 2.

approaches.name: Greedy Backward Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Start from the last index and move backward, updating the target to the current index if it is reachable. This greedy method checks if the start index can become the target.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Jump Game with Greedy Backward Scan
ladders.desc: Determine if you can reach the last index starting from first index using array values as max jump lengths.
ladders.explanation: The greedy backward scan approach solves the Jump Game problem by working from the end of the array toward the beginning. The key insight is that we don't need to track all possible paths or use dynamic programming with memoization. Instead, we maintain a 'target' variable representing the index we need to reach. Starting with target = n-1 (last index), we scan backward through the array. For each index i, we check if we can jump from i to the current target. If nums[i] >= target - i, then from position i we can reach the target. When this condition is true, we update target = i, meaning now we only need to reach index i instead of the original target. After scanning all elements, if target == 0, we know we can reach the last index from the first. The intuition is that if we can reach the current target from some earlier position, then that earlier position becomes our new target - we've effectively reduced the problem size. This works because we're finding the earliest position that can reach our current target, and if we can eventually work our way back to index 0, we have a valid path. Time complexity is O(n) since we make one pass through the array. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is optimal because any position that can reach the target is a valid stepping stone, and by greedily updating to the earliest such position, we ensure we find a path if one exists. Common mistakes include incorrect inequality (using > instead of >=), not updating target correctly, or misunderstanding that nums[i] represents maximum jump length, not exact jump length. The backward direction is crucial because it allows us to maintain a single target rather than tracking multiple possibilities.

examples.trace: Initialize target = 4 (last index). i=3: nums[3]=1, target-i=4-3=1, 1>=1 true → target=3. i=2: nums[2]=1, target-i=3-2=1, 1>=1 true → target=2. i=1: nums[1]=3, target-i=2-1=1, 3>=1 true → target=1. i=0: nums[0]=2, target-i=1-0=1, 2>=1 true → target=0. Final target=0 → return true.

Initialize target = 4 (last index). i=3: nums[3]=0, target-i=4-3=1, 0>=1 false → target remains 4. i=2: nums[2]=1, target-i=4-2=2, 1>=2 false → target remains 4. i=1: nums[1]=2, target-i=4-1=3, 2>=3 false → target remains 4. i=0: nums[0]=3, target-i=4-0=4, 3>=4 false → target remains 4. Final target=4 ≠ 0 → return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Can Reach Target Check
ladders.desc: Check if from current position you can reach a target position given max jump length.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core decision-making component of the greedy backward scan. Given a current index i with maximum jump length nums[i], and a target index, we need to determine if we can jump from i to reach the target. The mathematical condition is: nums[i] >= target - i. Here's why: The distance from i to target is target - i. Since nums[i] represents the maximum number of steps we can jump from position i, we can reach the target if our maximum jump length is at least the distance to the target. For example, if we're at index 2 with nums[2]=3 and target is index 5, distance = 5-2=3, and 3>=3 is true, so we can reach it. If nums[2]=2, then 2>=3 is false, so we cannot reach it. The intuition is straightforward: you can jump to any index within your maximum range. This check is performed at each position during the backward scan. When true, we update our target to the current position i, effectively saying 'now we only need to reach i instead of the original target.' This reduction is what makes the greedy approach work. Time complexity for this check is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being the decision criterion at each step of the backward scan. Common mistakes include using strict inequality (> instead of >=), forgetting that nums[i] is maximum jump (not exact), or miscalculating the distance. The check must account for the fact that we can jump exactly the distance needed, not just more than it.

examples.trace: Distance = target - i = 5-2=3. Check: nums[i] >= distance → 3 >= 3 → true. Therefore can reach target from position 2.
Distance = target - i = 4-1=3. Check: nums[i] >= distance → 2 >= 3 → false. Therefore cannot reach target from position 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Target Reduction
ladders.desc: Maintain and update a target index while scanning backward through array.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the greedy backward scan is maintaining a 'target' variable that represents the index we currently need to reach. We initialize target to the last index (n-1). Then we iterate backward from n-2 down to 0. At each position i, we check if we can jump from i to the current target using the condition from the sub-routine. If true, we update target = i. This update is the greedy choice: we're saying that if we can reach our current target from i, then our new problem is to reach i instead. By always updating to the current i when possible, we find the leftmost position that can reach our target, which maximizes our chances of eventually reaching index 0. The intuition is reduction: each time we update target, we're reducing the problem to a smaller subproblem (reach a closer index). After the loop, if target == 0, it means we can reduce all the way to the start, so return true. Otherwise return false. This works because if there exists any path from 0 to n-1, then by backward induction, we'll be able to reduce target to 0. The greedy choice is safe because any position that can reach the target is a valid intermediate point; choosing the current i (which is the rightmost such position in backward scan) doesn't miss solutions. Time complexity is O(n) for the backward scan. Space is O(1). This is the central technique that makes the full algorithm efficient compared to DP approaches. Common mistakes include scanning forward instead of backward, not initializing target correctly, or updating target when shouldn't. The backward direction ensures we always know our target is reachable from the end.

examples.trace: Start target=3. i=2: nums[2]=0, can reach target? distance=3-2=1, 0>=1 false → target stays 3. i=1: nums[1]=2, distance=3-1=2, 2>=2 true → target=1. i=0: nums[0]=1, distance=1-0=1, 1>=1 true → target=0. Final target=0.
Start target=2. i=1: nums[1]=1, distance=2-1=1, 1>=1 true → target=1. i=0: nums[0]=0, distance=1-0=1, 0>=1 false → target stays 1. Final target=1 (not 0).

ladders.title: Building Block — Backward Array Traversal
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array from the last element to the first.
ladders.explanation: Backward array traversal is a fundamental programming pattern where we process array elements in reverse order, starting from the last index and moving toward the first. In the context of the Jump Game greedy solution, this backward traversal is essential because it allows us to work from the destination backward to the source. We initialize a variable (often 'target') to the last index, then for i from n-2 down to 0, we perform operations. The loop typically uses: for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): or similar. The key insight is that by starting at the end, we know our ultimate goal (reach index n-1), and we try to find positions that can reach this goal, then make those positions our new sub-goals. This is opposite to forward traversal where we'd start at the beginning and try to reach the end. Backward traversal enables the greedy reduction because at each step, we know exactly what index we need to reach (the current target). If we traversed forward, we'd have multiple possible targets to track. The time complexity is O(n) for visiting each element once. Space is O(1). This building block connects to the core logic by providing the scanning mechanism for the greedy target reduction. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one errors), using forward traversal, or not decrementing properly. Understanding backward traversal is crucial for many algorithms including this greedy approach and various dynamic programming solutions.

examples.trace: Start with i=4 (if processing all elements). For backward traversal from second last to first: i=3, then i=2, then i=1, then i=0. In our algorithm, we start target at last index (4), then process i=3,2,1,0 in that order.
Initialize target=2. Then process i=1, then i=0. This visits indices in reverse order excluding the last which is our initial target.

testCases.input: n=3 with loop from n-2 to 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Distance Calculation
ladders.desc: Access array elements and compute differences between indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two fundamental skills: accessing elements in an array by their index, and calculating the distance between two indices. Array indexing is the process of retrieving the value at a specific position. In most programming languages, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. For an array nums of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Accessing nums[i] gives the value at position i. Distance calculation between two indices i and j is simply the absolute difference: |j - i|. In the Jump Game context, we need the distance from current index i to target index: target - i (since target >= i in backward scan). This tells us how many steps we need to jump. For example, if i=2 and target=5, distance = 5-2=3. Then we compare this distance with nums[i] (the maximum jump length). These operations are O(1) time and space. They form the building blocks for the 'can reach target' check. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indexing, using 1-based indexing incorrectly, or miscalculating distance (e.g., using i-target instead of target-i). It's crucial to understand that array indices are integers and distances are non-negative. This operation connects to the building block because backward traversal uses indexing to access each element, and distance calculation is needed for the reachability check.

examples.trace: Access nums[2]: value at index 2 is 8. Distance from i=2 to target=3: 3-2=1. So we have nums[i]=8 and distance=1.
Access nums[0]: value at index 0 is 1. Distance from i=0 to target=2: 2-0=2. So nums[i]=1, distance=2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Understand what an array is and how to use indices to access elements.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index. Think of it like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0. Each box can hold a value (like a number). The index is the position number of the box. For example, in array nums = [5, 3, 8], nums[0]=5 (first box), nums[1]=3 (second box), nums[2]=8 (third box). The length of an array is the number of elements it contains. Here length=3, so valid indices are 0, 1, 2. Accessing nums[4] would be invalid (out of bounds). Arrays are zero-indexed in most programming languages, meaning counting starts at 0. This is a crucial concept because all array operations depend on correct indexing. In the context of Jump Game, the array represents maximum jump lengths at each position. Understanding arrays and indices is the absolute prerequisite for any array algorithm. Time to access an element by index is O(1). Space for the array is O(n) where n is length. This foundation connects to basic operations because we need to access array elements using indices and understand distances between indices. Common beginner mistakes include thinking arrays are 1-indexed (starting at 1), confusing index with value, or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Practice by creating arrays and accessing elements at different positions.

examples.trace: The array has 3 elements. Index 0 refers to first element: 10. Index 1 refers to second element: 20. Index 2 refers to third element: 30. There is no index 3.
The array has 1 element. The only valid index is 0, which gives value 7. Length is 1.
